insights.title: Bank credit dipped to ₹212.1L Cr — first decline in 6 periods
insights.body: Total bank credit fell to ₹212.1 lakh crore in May 2026, down ₹1.5 lakh crore from the April record of ₹213.6 lakh crore. Volume contracted ₹213.6L Cr → ₹212.1L Cr, the first month-on-month decline after five consecutive periods of expansion from ₹178.7L Cr in February 2025.
insights.implication: This is a seasonal correction, not a trend break — May typically sees loan repayments and lower disbursements post-year-end. Watch June for confirmation of resumed growth or structural slowdown.

insights.title: FY26 acceleration jumped to 7.1pp — highest on record
insights.body: Bank credit growth accelerated 7.1 percentage points from FY25 to FY26, the maximum acceleration in this dataset. Acceleration surged from 5.1pp → 7.1pp, a 2-point jump that breaks the six-period plateau at 5.1pp.
insights.implication: The full-year growth rate has re-accelerated sharply, confirming FY26 as a structural inflection year. Banks are in a higher growth regime, not just riding a cyclical wave.

insights.title: Banks added ₹31.2L Cr in FY26 — largest annual expansion
insights.body: Banks deployed ₹31.2 lakh crore in absolute credit during FY26, the highest annual addition on record. FY26 credit creation rose from ₹29.6L Cr → ₹31.2L Cr, adding ₹1.6 lakh crore to the prior peak.
insights.implication: This is the largest single-year credit expansion in the dataset, demonstrating banks' capacity to sustain large-scale lending even as the base grows. Expect this to anchor FY27 growth expectations.

insights.title: Bank credit at 16.0% YoY — holding near peak despite May dip
insights.body: Bank credit grew 16.0% year-on-year in May 2026, virtually unchanged from April's 16.1%. Growth held steady at 16.1% → 16.0%, a negligible 0.08-point decline despite the absolute volume contraction.
insights.implication: YoY growth remains at the upper end of its historical range, confirming the May dip is a base effect, not a slowdown. Credit momentum is intact.

insights.title: Food credit surged to ₹1.04L Cr — new all-time high
insights.body: Food credit outstanding jumped to ₹1.04 lakh crore in May 2026, the highest level in this dataset. Volume spiked ₹70,271 Cr → ₹1,03,915 Cr, a ₹33,644 crore increase reversing April's seasonal decline.
insights.implication: This reflects the rabi procurement cycle kicking in — banks are financing government food purchases and agricultural working capital. Expect this to moderate post-procurement.

insights.title: Food credit at 66.4% YoY — still elevated despite sharp deceleration
insights.body: Food credit grew 66.4% year-on-year in May 2026, down from April's 139.4%. Growth decelerated sharply from 139.4% → 66.4%, a 73-point drop as the prior-year base normalized.
insights.implication: Despite the deceleration, food credit is still growing at double the system rate, driven by policy support for agricultural lending. The April spike was a base anomaly, not a sustainable trend.

insights.title: Non-food credit fell to ₹211.1L Cr — first decline in 6 periods
insights.body: Non-food credit dropped to ₹211.1 lakh crore in May 2026, down ₹1.8 lakh crore from April's record. Volume contracted ₹212.9L Cr → ₹211.1L Cr, the first month-on-month decline after five consecutive periods of expansion from ₹178.1L Cr in February 2025.
insights.implication: This mirrors the total credit dip and is seasonal — non-food credit drives 99% of system volume, so any system-level correction shows up here. Core lending momentum remains strong.

insights.title: Non-food credit added ₹30.8L Cr in FY26 — record expansion
insights.body: Non-food credit added ₹30.8 lakh crore during FY26, the maximum annual expansion in this dataset. FY26 addition rose from ₹29.2L Cr → ₹30.8L Cr, adding ₹1.6 lakh crore to the prior peak.
insights.implication: Non-food credit is the engine of system growth, with virtually all incremental lending flowing into industrial, services, and retail segments. This sets a high bar for FY27.

insights.title: Non-food credit at 15.8% YoY — stable despite May contraction
insights.body: Non-food credit grew 15.8% year-on-year in May 2026, nearly flat from April's 15.9%. Growth held steady at 15.9% → 15.8%, a negligible 0.06-point decline despite the absolute volume drop.
insights.implication: YoY growth remains at the upper end of its historical range, confirming the May dip is a calendar effect, not a structural slowdown. Lending momentum is intact across commercial and retail segments.

insights.title: All 4 sectors growing YoY — breadth unchanged for 7 periods
insights.body: All 4 main sectors recorded positive year-on-year growth in May 2026, unchanged from prior periods. Breadth remained steady at 4 sectors across all seven reporting periods from February 2025 through May 2026.
insights.implication: Growth is broad-based across agriculture, industry, services, and retail — no sector is contracting, and the expansion is not concentrated in a single segment. This is a system-wide credit cycle.

insights.title: Agriculture holds 12.44% of credit — stable at median
insights.body: Agriculture accounts for 12.44% of total bank credit in May 2026. Share ticked up marginally from 12.43% → 12.44%, essentially flat and near historical median.
insights.implication: Agriculture maintains its traditional ~12.4% allocation despite double-digit growth — banks are expanding ag lending in lockstep with overall portfolio growth, not overweighting the sector.

insights.title: Agriculture growth slowed to 13.7% from 15.7% peak
insights.body: Agriculture credit grew 13.70% year-on-year in May 2026. Growth decelerated from 15.70% → 13.70%, a 2 percentage point pullback from the prior period's record high.
insights.implication: Agriculture has come off its peak but remains in the upper range of this dataset — the slowdown suggests seasonal moderation after a strong crop cycle, not a structural reversal.

insights.title: Industry FY acceleration at 6.8pp — new record high
insights.body: Industry credit shows full-year acceleration of 6.8 percentage points in May 2026. FY acceleration edged up from 6.7pp → 6.8pp, breaking the six-period plateau to reach a new maximum.
insights.implication: Industry lending momentum has strengthened further on a full-year basis — this is the strongest structural acceleration on record, signaling sustained capex and manufacturing recovery.

insights.title: Industry share at 21.50% — lowest in dataset
insights.body: Industry accounts for 21.50% of total credit in May 2026. Share edged down from 21.52% → 21.50%, holding at the minimum level in this 6-period window.
insights.implication: Despite record growth rates, industry is losing portfolio share — other sectors (personal loans, services) are growing even faster, reshaping the credit mix away from traditional industrial lending.

insights.title: Industry credit at 15.06% YoY — highest on record
insights.body: Industry credit grew 15.06% year-on-year in May 2026, the strongest pace in this dataset. Growth accelerated marginally from 14.96% → 15.06%, extending the upward trajectory from 8.2% in April 2025.
insights.implication: Industry credit is at peak growth momentum — infrastructure investment, manufacturing revival, and corporate capex are driving the strongest lending cycle in this window.

insights.title: Personal loans at 33.0% share — highest in dataset
insights.body: Personal loans represent 32.98% of total credit in May 2026. Share jumped from 32.60% → 32.98%, a 0.37 percentage point gain to reach the upper range of this window.
insights.implication: Personal loans have reversed the prior period's decline and now command the largest sector allocation — consumer lending is regaining momentum and reshaping the credit mix toward retail borrowers.

insights.title: Personal loans at 16.0% YoY — upper range sustained
insights.body: Personal loans credit grew 16.01% year-on-year in May 2026. Growth eased marginally from 16.24% → 16.01%, staying in the upper range after climbing from 11.7% in April 2025.
insights.implication: Consumer lending momentum remains strong despite the slight deceleration — household credit demand has turned durably positive, supporting retail-led credit expansion.

insights.title: Services FY acceleration at 7.0pp — maximum sustained
insights.body: Services credit maintained 7.0 percentage point full-year acceleration in May 2026. FY acceleration held steady at 7.0pp for seven consecutive periods since February 2025, the maximum level on record.
insights.implication: Services credit growth has structurally shifted higher and sustained the pace for over a year — this is an entrenched uptrend driven by broad-based economic activity beyond manufacturing.

insights.title: Services at 28.2% share — upper range after pullback
insights.body: Services captured 28.21% of total bank credit in May 2026. Share declined from 28.47% → 28.21%, pulling back from the prior period's peak but remaining in the upper range.
insights.implication: Services ceded some ground to personal loans but still command a historically high allocation — banks continue to favor services lending as the sector outgrows traditional industry credit.

insights.title: Services credit 18.6% YoY — growth decelerating from 19.0% peak
insights.body: Services credit grew 18.58% year-on-year in May 2026, down 0.41 points from the prior period's 18.99%. Growth peaked at 19.0% in April 2026 and has now eased to 18.6%, the first deceleration after three consecutive periods of acceleration from 15.5% in February 2026.
insights.implication: Services remains the fastest-growing major sector, but the momentum break after hitting 19% signals the credit cycle may be maturing. Watch for sustained deceleration — if growth falls below 16%, the expansion phase is ending.

insights.title: Large corporates hold 67.15% of industry credit — stable at equilibrium
insights.body: Large corporates hold a 67.15% share of industry credit, up 0.01 points from the prior period. The share has stabilized at 67.1% → 67.2% after declining from 71.0% → 67.1% over the past year.
insights.implication: The corporate credit market has found equilibrium. Large enterprises maintain dominant access to bank financing, but the year-long decline confirms banks are systematically expanding to smaller players.

insights.title: Large corporate credit at 10.04% YoY — highest on record
insights.body: Large corporate credit growth accelerated to 10.04%, the strongest reading in this 7-period window. Growth momentum built from 8.95% → 10.04%, breaking above the prior maximum of 8.9%.
insights.implication: Banks are backing large corporate capex plans with renewed confidence. This acceleration to double digits signals corporate India's investment cycle is gaining momentum with institutional financing support — the strongest signal yet.

insights.title: Medium enterprises at 9.73% share — highest on record
insights.body: Medium enterprises hold a 9.73% share of industry credit, up 0.08 points from the prior period. The share climbed from 9.65% → 9.73%, reaching the highest level in this dataset after rising from 8.9% a year ago.
insights.implication: Medium enterprises have secured a record 1-in-10 share of industry financing. The continued climb signals banks are finding sustainable allocation to this growth-critical segment beyond policy mandates.

insights.title: Medium enterprise credit at 19.88% YoY — decelerating from peak
insights.body: Medium enterprise credit growth reached 19.88%, down 1.86 points from the prior period. Growth momentum decelerated from 21.74% → 19.88%, pulling back from the recent peak of 22.3%.
insights.implication: Medium enterprise lending remains strong at near-20% but is cooling from the aggressive 21%+ pace. Banks may be consolidating recent gains or facing supply constraints in this segment after sustained expansion.

insights.title: Micro & Small FY acceleration at 23.8pp — slight moderation from peak
insights.body: Micro & Small enterprise credit posted 23.8pp full-year acceleration, down 0.5 points from the prior period. Acceleration eased from 24.2pp → 23.8pp, the first decline after holding at maximum for six consecutive periods.
insights.implication: This signals entrenched momentum in small business lending, not a temporary spike. The marginal pullback from 24.2pp suggests the explosive growth phase may be stabilizing, but the 23.8pp acceleration still reflects systematic expansion of micro-enterprise exposure.

insights.title: Micro & Small at 23.12% share — holding near record high
insights.body: Micro & Small enterprises claimed 23.12% of total industry credit, down 0.08 points from the prior period. Share held steady at 23.20% → 23.12%, near the peak of 23.5% reached in February 2026 after climbing from 20.0% a year ago.
insights.implication: Small businesses are winning a structurally larger slice of bank credit allocation. The stability at 23%+ reflects deliberate portfolio rebalancing toward smaller ticket lending, now embedded in bank strategy.

insights.title: Micro & Small credit at 30.09% YoY — decelerating from 33.14% peak
insights.body: Micro & Small enterprise credit surged 30.09% year-on-year, down 3.05 points from the prior period. Growth decelerated from 33.14% → 30.09%, pulling back from the record high but still 3× the 9.6% rate a year ago.
insights.implication: Banks are consolidating after the explosive 33%+ surge. The 30% pace still signals aggressive small business book expansion, but the deceleration suggests banks are digesting recent growth or facing origination capacity limits.

insights.title: MSME outpaces large corporates by 20.1pp — gap narrowing from 24.2pp
insights.body: MSME credit outpaced large corporate lending by 20.1 percentage points, down 4.1 points from the prior period. The growth gap narrowed from 24.2pp → 20.1pp as large corporate growth accelerated to 10.0% while MSME growth decelerated to 30.1%.
insights.implication: Banks are rebalancing toward large corporates after favoring smaller borrowers. The narrowing spread reflects large corporate capex revival catching up with MSME momentum — a sign of broad-based industrial credit expansion rather than small-ticket concentration.

insights.title: HFCs lead at 17.9%, PFIs at 16.9% — gap narrowing
insights.body: Housing Finance Companies command 17.9% of NBFC credit while Public Financial Institutions hold 16.9%. PFIs shifted from strengthening to active status, with a 1.0 percentage point gap between the two segments — down from 1.2pp last period.
insights.implication: The NBFC sector shows balanced allocation between housing and public finance, with PFIs closing the gap on HFCs — watch for PFIs to challenge HFC dominance as their growth momentum persists.

insights.title: PFIs surging at 57.1% YoY, HFCs at 16.7% — 40pp spread
insights.body: Public Financial Institutions are growing at 57.1% while Housing Finance Companies expand at 16.7%. Both segments are in strengthening status, with a massive 40.4 percentage point growth spread — up from 37pp last period.
insights.implication: This is an extreme divergence — PFIs are in hypergrowth mode, likely driven by infrastructure and development finance mandates, while HFCs face structural headwinds from regulatory tightening or market saturation.

insights.title: NBFCs lead at 34.5%, Trade weakens to 22.1%
insights.body: NBFCs command 34.5% of services credit, Trade holds 22.1%, and Other Services 20.5%. NBFCs shifted from strengthening to active status, while Trade moved from active to weakening — the spread between top and bottom remains 34.4 percentage points.
insights.implication: The NBFC boom continues to drive services credit expansion, but Trade is losing momentum — banks are funding non-bank lenders who reach underserved segments, while traditional trade finance faces headwinds.

insights.title: Computer Software 52.7% YoY — now strengthening, Shipping cools to 40.7%
insights.body: Computer Software leads services growth at 52.7% and advanced to strengthening status, followed by Shipping at 40.7% (now active) and NBFCs at 27.7% (strengthening). Computer Software surged from 39.0% to 52.7% and shifted from active to strengthening. Shipping decelerated from 42.2% to 40.7% and dropped back to active. Four sub-sectors that were strengthening last period — Shipping, Tourism, Commercial Real Estate, Trade — have all reverted to active.
insights.implication: Growth is concentrating in tech financing while infrastructure-linked sectors cool. The broad-based strengthening seen last period has reversed — only Computer Software, NBFCs, and Professional Services are strengthening now. This is a narrowing, not a broadening, of the services credit boom.

insights.title: Retail Trade 44.4% share — now strengthening, Wholesale slips to weakening
insights.body: Retail Trade holds 44.4% of trade credit and strengthened, while Wholesale Trade's 55.6% share weakened. The status positions flipped: Wholesale Trade moved from strengthening to weakening, Retail Trade from weakening to strengthening. The 11.1 percentage point spread is unchanged, but the momentum has reversed.
insights.implication: Consumer retail is gaining share momentum for the first time in this window, while B2B wholesale financing is losing ground. If this persists, it signals a shift from supply-side inventory build to demand-side consumer spending — a healthier credit mix for sustained growth.

insights.title: Wholesale Trade 18.1% YoY — decelerated sharply, Retail 8.0% steady
insights.body: Wholesale Trade grew 18.1% and Retail Trade 8.0%, both holding active status with a 10.1 percentage point growth gap. Wholesale Trade decelerated from 20.1% to 18.1%, while Retail Trade slowed from 11.5% to 8.0%. Both shifted from strengthening to active, but wholesale's 2-point drop is larger than retail's 3.5-point decline in absolute terms.
insights.implication: Trade credit is cooling across both channels, but wholesale is decelerating faster — consistent with the share shift toward retail. The 10-point growth gap remains wide, but if wholesale continues to slow while retail stabilizes, the trade credit mix will rebalance toward consumer-facing activity.

insights.title: Credit cards sub-10% YoY for 4 straight periods — record streak
insights.body: Credit card growth has stayed below 10% for 4 consecutive periods, the longest such streak on record. Streak extended from 3 periods to 4 periods, reaching a new maximum.
insights.implication: Credit card demand remains in a sustained weak phase with no recovery signal — consumer spending restraint is now entrenched, not cyclical.

insights.title: Credit card share edges up to 4.28% — first gain in 5 periods
insights.body: Credit cards now represent 4.28% of personal loans, up from 4.24% in the prior period. Share ticked up 4.24% → 4.28%, reversing a year-long decline that bottomed at 4.2%.
insights.implication: The share erosion has paused, but credit cards remain near their lowest portfolio weight — recovery depends on sustained acceleration in absolute growth.

insights.title: Credit card growth at 3.78% YoY — second consecutive uptick
insights.body: Credit card outstanding grew 3.78% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 3.55% in April. Growth improved 3.55% → 3.78%, continuing a gradual recovery from the 1.5% trough in February 2026.
insights.implication: Credit cards are stabilising but remain in single-digit growth territory — the sector has not yet broken out of its post-correction phase.

insights.title: Consumer durables at 0.32% share — marginal and stable
insights.body: Consumer durables represent 0.32% of personal loans, unchanged from the prior period. Share has held flat at 0.3% for three consecutive periods after dropping from 0.4% a year ago.
insights.implication: Consumer durables have become a negligible segment within personal lending, with no growth catalyst visible.

insights.title: Education loans at 2.24% share — lowest in this window
insights.body: Education loans account for 2.24% of personal loans, down slightly from 2.25% in the prior period. Share edged down 2.25% → 2.24%, reaching the minimum level in this 7-period range.
insights.implication: Education loans maintain a stable but small share of personal lending, with marginal erosion as faster-growing segments like gold loans gain ground.

insights.title: Education loans at 13.27% YoY — steady double-digit growth
insights.body: Education loans grew 13.27% year-on-year, down marginally from 13.38% in the prior period. Growth has gradually decelerated 15.9% → 13.3% over the past year but remains in double digits.
insights.implication: Education loans are the most consistent performer in personal lending, delivering stable double-digit growth as families prioritize skill investment despite economic uncertainty.

insights.title: FD advances at 2.27% share — down from April peak
insights.body: Advances against Fixed Deposits represent 2.27% of personal loans, down from 2.44% in the prior period. Share dropped 2.44% → 2.27%, retreating from the highest level in this dataset.
insights.implication: FD-backed advances have pulled back from their peak share, suggesting the April surge was a one-period spike rather than a sustained shift.

insights.title: FD advances at 9.61% YoY — sharp deceleration from 19.22%
insights.body: FD-backed advances grew 9.61% year-on-year, down sharply from 19.22% in the prior period. Growth collapsed 19.22% → 9.61%, erasing the entire acceleration and falling to the lowest level in this window.
insights.implication: The April surge in FD-backed lending was a one-off spike, not a trend — growth has reverted to single digits, signaling banks are not aggressively expanding this product.

insights.title: Gold loans positive for 8 straight periods — longest run on record
insights.body: Gold loans have grown every period for 8 consecutive months, the longest unbroken run in this dataset. Positive streak extended from 6 periods to 8 periods, setting a new maximum.
insights.implication: Gold lending has become the most reliable growth engine in personal loans, with no signs of momentum breaking — this is the strongest structural trend in retail credit.

insights.title: Gold loans hit 7.02% share — highest on record, up 0.39pp
insights.body: Gold loans captured 7.02% of all personal lending, the highest level in this dataset. Share jumped 6.63% → 7.02%, continuing a dramatic climb from 3.0% a year ago.
insights.implication: Gold loans have more than doubled their portfolio weight in 12 months, reshaping the personal lending mix toward secured products — this is a structural shift, not a cyclical swing.

insights.title: Gold loans at 121.2% YoY — still doubling despite cooling
insights.body: Gold loan growth held at 121.21%, easing marginally from 123.15% last period. Growth decelerated 123.15% → 121.21%, down 1.94 points but still more than doubling year-on-year volumes.
insights.implication: Gold lending remains in hypergrowth mode despite the slight cooling. Banks continue to aggressively expand this secured product as borrowers pledge jewelry for credit access, sustaining the longest boom in this dataset.

insights.title: Housing share drops to 48.2% — lowest on record
insights.body: Housing loans fell to 48.15% of personal lending, the smallest share in this 7-period window. Portfolio weight declined 48.35% → 48.15%, extending the slide from over 50% a year ago.
insights.implication: Housing's traditional dominance continues eroding as faster-growing secured products like gold and vehicles capture market share. The structural shift away from mortgage concentration is accelerating.

insights.title: Housing growth steady at 11.4% — near upper range
insights.body: Housing loan growth held at 11.39%, essentially flat from 11.48% last period. Growth eased marginally 11.48% → 11.39%, staying near the top of its 10.7-11.5% range.
insights.implication: Despite losing share, housing credit is expanding at a healthy pace. Underlying mortgage demand remains robust, but other secured products are simply growing faster.

insights.title: Other Personal Loans at 24.9% — record low share
insights.body: Other personal loans fell to 24.90% of the portfolio, the smallest share in this dataset. Share declined 24.99% → 24.90%, continuing the gradual slide from 25.8% levels.
insights.implication: Miscellaneous unsecured lending is steadily ceding ground to specialized secured products as banks tighten risk appetite and borrowers shift to collateral-backed credit.

insights.title: Other Personal Loans at 12.7% YoY — near peak growth
insights.body: Other Personal Loans grew 12.70% year-on-year, near the top of this period's range. Growth eased 12.98% → 12.70%, down 0.28 points but still well above the 7.5% low from April 2025.
insights.implication: Non-housing, non-vehicle personal credit maintains strong momentum despite losing share. Banks are expanding general consumer financing, but secured categories are simply outpacing it.

insights.title: Advances against shares at 0.15% — minimal penetration
insights.body: Advances against shares and bonds held 0.15% of total personal loans, unchanged from last period. Share remained flat at 0.15%, fluctuating within a narrow 0.1-0.2% band across all periods.
insights.implication: Securities-backed lending remains a niche product with negligible market penetration despite recent volatility in growth rates.

insights.title: Housing 48.2%, Other PL 24.9%, Vehicle 10.7% — top three dominate
insights.body: Housing dominates personal loans at 48.2% share, followed by Other Personal Loans at 24.9% and Vehicle Loans at 10.7%. The top three categories control 83.8% of the market, with niche segments like advances against shares (0.1%) and consumer durables (0.3%) representing minimal share.
insights.implication: Personal credit remains concentrated in housing and general-purpose loans, with secured vehicle financing the only other material segment. Limited product diversification persists across banks, though gold's rapid rise is beginning to reshape the mix.

insights.title: Vehicle loans hold 10.7% share — stable amid growth
insights.body: Vehicle loans held 10.67% of personal loans, essentially unchanged from 10.64% last period. Share edged up marginally 10.64% → 10.67%, staying within the tight 10.4-10.7% range.
insights.implication: Vehicle lending share is stable despite strong growth, indicating the broader personal loans pool is expanding at a similar pace. Auto financing is keeping pace with the overall market.

insights.title: Vehicle loans at 18.0% YoY — near peak growth
insights.body: Vehicle loans grew 17.97% year-on-year, near the top of this period's range. Growth eased 18.61% → 17.97%, down 0.63 points but still more than double the 8.6% low from April 2025.
insights.implication: Auto financing remains in a pronounced upturn as consumer demand for vehicles stays strong and banks compete aggressively in this secured lending segment.

gaps.title: Consumer durables negative for 6 straight periods — record contraction
gaps.body: Consumer durables lending has contracted for 6 consecutive periods, the longest negative streak in this dataset. Negative streak jumped from 4 periods to 6 periods, setting a new maximum.
gaps.implication: Consumer durables financing is in structural decline — discretionary spending on cars and appliances shows no signs of recovery despite broader credit growth.

gaps.title: Consumer durables -3.38% YoY — contraction easing but still negative
gaps.body: Consumer durables lending contracted 3.38% year-on-year, an improvement from the 5.34% decline in April. Contraction moderated -5.34% → -3.38%, the least severe decline in four periods.
gaps.implication: The pace of decline is slowing, but consumer durables remain in contraction — the segment is stabilising at a lower base, not recovering.

gaps.title: Advances against shares turn negative at -3.6% — trend reversal
gaps.body: Advances against shares and bonds contracted 3.59% year-on-year, the first decline in this dataset. Growth reversed sharply from +4.63% → -3.59%, an 8.22 point drop and a complete trend break.
gaps.implication: Securities-backed lending has flipped from recovery to contraction. The volatile swing from 33% growth in early 2025 to negative territory signals banks are pulling back from this collateral type amid market uncertainty.

gaps.title: Gold 121.2%, Vehicle 18.0%, Education 13.3% lead — durables contract
gaps.body: Gold loans exploded 121.2% year-on-year, dwarfing all other personal loan categories, while Consumer Durables contracted 3.4% and Advances against shares fell 3.6%. The growth spread widened to 124.8 percentage points between gold loans and advances against shares, with Vehicle Loans (18.0%) and Education (13.3%) also showing strong momentum. Status shifts: Advances against shares flipped from strengthening to declining.
gaps.implication: Personal credit is polarising sharply — secured lending against gold and vehicles is booming while unsecured consumer financing and securities-backed lending contract. Banks are doubling down on collateral-backed exposure, with gold jewelry emerging as the preferred asset class amid economic uncertainty.

insights.title: PSL agriculture at 17.3% YoY — highest on record
insights.body: PSL agriculture credit grew 17.33% year-on-year in May 2026, the strongest pace in this 7-period window. Growth accelerated from 16.03% → 17.33%, extending a steady climb from 11.0% in April 2025.
insights.implication: Rural credit demand is strengthening as agricultural income improves and monsoon expectations support both seasonal working capital and farm mechanisation lending. Banks should expand agri-focused origination capacity.

insights.title: PSL education loans hold at 23.3% YoY — demand sustained
insights.body: PSL educational loans expanded 23.27% year-on-year, maintaining near-peak growth with minimal change from prior period. Growth held steady at 23.02% → 23.27%, sustaining the surge from near-zero levels a year ago.
insights.implication: Student loan demand remains robust as higher education costs rise, with no signs of underwriting tightening despite the rapid expansion. This is a stable high-growth segment.

insights.title: PSL housing at 39.9% YoY — boom continues at peak
insights.body: PSL housing credit surged 39.94% year-on-year, holding at the highest growth rate in this window. Growth edged up from 39.80% → 39.94%, sustaining the dramatic turnaround from negative territory a year ago.
insights.implication: Affordable housing demand is in full boom as interest rates stabilise and government incentives drive first-time buyer activity. This is the single strongest PSL growth story — prioritise origination and distribution capacity.

insights.title: PSL medium enterprises at 17.2% YoY — slowest on record
insights.body: PSL medium enterprise credit grew 17.24% year-on-year, marking the weakest pace in this 7-period window. Growth decelerated from 17.85% → 17.24%, continuing a steady decline from peaks above 22% in early 2025.
insights.implication: Mid-market businesses are facing sustained tightening as banks shift focus to retail and micro-enterprise lending with better risk profiles. Medium enterprises are losing priority in PSL allocation.

insights.title: PSL MSME acceleration at 15.9pp — first decline from peak
insights.body: PSL MSME credit acceleration measured 15.9 percentage points on a full-year basis, the first drop from the sustained 16.1pp level. Acceleration eased from 16.1pp → 15.9pp, breaking a 6-period streak at maximum.
insights.implication: MSME lending momentum remains structurally elevated but is beginning to moderate from peak acceleration. The shift is marginal — government guarantee schemes and digital platforms continue to support strong growth, but the inflection point may be near.

insights.title: PSL micro & small at 28.5% YoY — cooling from peak
insights.body: PSL micro and small enterprise credit expanded 28.53% year-on-year, easing from the prior period's record high. Growth decelerated from 29.53% → 28.53%, still more than double the 13.0% level a year ago.
insights.implication: Small business credit remains in a lending boom driven by digital platforms and government guarantees, but the first deceleration signals banks may be approaching capacity or tightening underwriting after rapid expansion.

insights.title: Renewable energy positive for 8 straight periods — record streak
insights.body: Renewable energy lending has grown for 8 consecutive periods, the longest positive streak on record. Extended from 6 to 8 periods, reaching a new historical maximum despite recent growth moderation.
insights.implication: Banks are sustaining their renewable energy push through multiple growth cycles, signaling structural commitment to green financing mandates. This is a durable trend despite volatility in growth rates.

insights.title: Renewable energy at 15.6% YoY — sharp deceleration to lowest
insights.body: Renewable energy lending grew 15.64% year-on-year, the weakest reading in this 7-period window. Growth decelerated sharply from 34.13% → 15.64%, falling 18.49 points and reaching the minimum level.
insights.implication: The green lending boom is cooling rapidly after explosive growth phases, but the 8-period positive streak and double-digit expansion suggest structural demand remains intact. Banks are normalising after an overheated phase.

insights.title: Social infrastructure surges to 35.3% YoY — sharp rebound
insights.body: Social infrastructure lending grew 35.27% year-on-year, surging from 12.88% in the prior period. Growth accelerated sharply by 22.39 points, from 12.88% → 35.27%, after swinging positive from contraction just two periods ago.
insights.implication: Banks are aggressively re-engaging with social infrastructure after a brief pullback, likely driven by government spending on healthcare and education facilities. This is a high-volatility segment with strong current momentum.

insights.title: Weaker sections at 10.7% YoY — weakest on record
insights.body: Weaker sections lending grew 10.67% year-on-year, the slowest pace in this 7-period window. Growth decelerated from 11.07% → 10.67%, continuing a downward trend from 15.3% in April 2025.
insights.implication: Financial inclusion momentum is softening as banks prioritise higher-yield PSL categories over mandated weaker section targets. This segment is losing priority in portfolio allocation.

insights.title: Housing 39.9%, Export Credit -12.0% — PSL spread widens to 52.4%
insights.body: Housing leads PSL growth at 39.9%, followed by Social Infrastructure at 35.3% and Micro and Small Enterprises at 28.5%, while Export Credit contracts 12.0% and Others decline 12.5%. Top-to-bottom spread widened from 49.4% to 52.4%. Micro and Small Enterprises upgraded from strengthening to active status, Educational Loans upgraded from active to strengthening. Export Credit contraction deepened from -8.4% → -12.0%, Others from -9.6% → -12.5%.
insights.implication: PSL bifurcation is intensifying — housing and infrastructure remain banker favorites while trade-linked credit deteriorates further. Micro enterprise momentum has crossed into sustained expansion territory, signaling structural shift in MSME credit appetite. Export credit weakness now exceeds double digits for the first time in this window, reflecting persistent external demand headwinds.

gaps.title: PSL export credit at -12.0% YoY — decline deepens again
gaps.body: PSL export credit contracted 12.02% year-on-year, reversing the prior period's improvement. Contraction widened from -8.36% → -12.02%, moving away from the recovery path that had narrowed losses from -17.2%.
gaps.implication: Export financing remains volatile and under pressure from global trade uncertainty. The reversal suggests banks' earlier optimism was premature — expect continued weakness in trade credit demand.

gaps.title: PSL others at -12.5% YoY — contraction widens again
gaps.body: PSL other categories contracted 12.45% year-on-year, reversing the prior period's sharp improvement. Contraction widened from -9.55% → -12.45%, moving away from the recovery path that had narrowed losses from -23.9%.
gaps.implication: Specialty PSL categories remain volatile and structurally weak as banks prioritise core agriculture, MSME, and housing segments. Expect continued portfolio rebalancing away from these categories.

insights.title: Iron & Steel share slips to 68.2% — Other Metal gains
insights.body: Iron & Steel holds 68.2% of basic metal credit while Other Metal Products commands 31.8%. Iron & Steel weakened from 68.8% → 68.2% while Other Metal Products strengthened from 31.2% → 31.8%, narrowing the spread from 37.7pp to 36.4pp.
insights.implication: The share shift confirms specialty metals are steadily eroding steel's dominance, likely driven by manufacturing diversification beyond core infrastructure. The trend is small but persistent.

insights.title: Other Metal Products at 19.9% YoY — outpaces Iron & Steel
insights.body: Other Metal Products grew 19.9% year-on-year versus Iron & Steel at 16.7%. Growth rates tightened further — spread narrowed from 2.2pp to 3.3pp, with Iron & Steel decelerating from 18.7% → 16.7% while Other Metal Products slowed from 21.0% → 19.9%.
insights.implication: Both segments are cooling but specialty metals maintain their growth edge. Iron & Steel's shift to active status signals momentum loss in traditional steel financing despite infrastructure demand.

insights.title: Electronics share rises to 19.9% — gains on Other Engineering
insights.body: Other Engineering holds 80.1% of engineering credit while Electronics commands 19.9%. Electronics strengthened from 18.9% → 19.9% while Other Engineering weakened from 81.1% → 80.1%, narrowing the gap from 62.2pp to 60.2pp.
insights.implication: Electronics is clawing back share from traditional engineering, likely reflecting semiconductor and hardware manufacturing policy push. The 1pp shift is the largest single-period move in this dataset.

insights.title: Other Engineering at 34.5% YoY — Electronics strengthens to 17.8%
insights.body: Other Engineering grew 34.5% year-on-year versus Electronics at 17.8%. Other Engineering decelerated sharply from 37.5% → 34.5% while Electronics accelerated from 13.3% → 17.8%, cutting the growth spread from 24.2pp to 16.7pp.
insights.implication: Electronics is gaining momentum after lagging — the shift to strengthening status confirms the sector is entering a new growth phase. Traditional engineering remains dominant but is losing its growth premium.

insights.title: Edible Oils share hits 11.1% — Sugar slips to match
insights.body: Others commands 75.9% of food processing credit, followed by Sugar and Edible Oils tied at 11.1%, and Tea at 1.9%. Edible Oils strengthened from 10.4% → 11.1% while Sugar weakened from 11.9% → 11.1%, creating a rare parity between the two segments.
insights.implication: Edible oils have overtaken sugar in credit allocation momentum, reflecting the shift toward processed food manufacturing. Sugar's weakening status signals structural headwinds in traditional commodity processing.

insights.title: Infrastructure holds 32.8% of industry credit — share stable
insights.body: Infrastructure dominates industry credit with a 32.8% share, followed by Basic Metal and Metal Products at 11.3% and Other Industries at 7.5%. Share composition remains stable with Infrastructure at 32.8% (vs 32.6% prior), Basic Metal at 11.3% (unchanged), and the bottom tier unchanged at Leather 0.3%, Glass 0.4%, Wood 0.7%.
insights.implication: Industry credit allocation is locked in — infrastructure and metals continue to absorb one-third of all industry lending. The stability signals banks are maintaining their capital formation bets rather than rotating toward new sectors.

insights.title: Power share rises to 56.8% — Telecom weakens further
insights.body: Power commands 56.8% of infrastructure credit, with Roads at 22.8% and Other Infrastructure at 12.2%. Power strengthened from 56.6% → 56.8% while Telecommunications weakened, with the top 3 categories controlling 91.8% of infrastructure credit (vs 91.2% prior).
insights.implication: Power sector credit concentration is intensifying as renewable energy and grid expansion drive lending. Telecom's shift to weakening status confirms banks are pulling back from the sector despite 5G rollout.

insights.title: Other Textiles dominates at 44.7% — Cotton at 36.5%
insights.body: Other Textiles dominates at 44.7% of textile credit, followed by Cotton Textiles at 36.5%, Man-Made Textiles at 17.1%, and Jute Textiles at 1.7%. All four textile sub-segments remain active with a 42.9% spread between largest and smallest, unchanged in structure from prior period.
insights.implication: Textile lending is concentrated in non-cotton segments, likely reflecting synthetic fiber and technical textile expansion over traditional cotton manufacturing. The stable distribution confirms this is a structural shift, not a cyclical reallocation.

insights.title: Jute Textiles leads at 19.9% YoY — all segments positive
insights.body: All four textile segments posted positive growth, led by Jute Textiles at 19.9% and Other Textiles at 13.3%, with Cotton Textiles at 4.8% and Man-Made Textiles at 2.7%. Jute Textiles upgraded to strengthening at 19.9%, up from 16.5% prior period. Other Textiles and Man-Made Textiles both downgraded from strengthening to active, indicating deceleration.
insights.implication: Even traditional manufacturing sectors like textiles are benefiting from the credit expansion. The universal positive growth signals broad industrial optimism, but the status downgrades in Other and Man-Made Textiles suggest momentum is concentrating in niche segments like Jute rather than mainstream synthetic fibers.

gaps.title: Drugs & Pharma share dips to 33.5% — Fertiliser stabilises
gaps.body: Others leads chemical credit at 44.1%, followed by Drugs & Pharmaceuticals at 33.5%, Petro Chemicals at 12.2%, and Fertiliser at 10.2%. Drugs & Pharmaceuticals weakened from 33.9% → 33.5% while Fertiliser stabilised from 9.9% → 10.2%, both shifting to active status from prior strengthening/weakening.
gaps.implication: The pharma credit allocation story is pausing after sustained gains. Fertiliser's stabilisation suggests the agricultural input credit contraction may be bottoming out.

gaps.title: Drugs & Pharma leads at 20.6% YoY — Fertiliser still negative
gaps.body: Drugs & Pharmaceuticals grew 20.6% year-on-year, Petro Chemicals at 19.4%, Others at 16.4%, while Fertiliser contracted -0.3%. Growth spread narrowed sharply from 32.2pp to 20.9pp as Drugs & Pharma accelerated from 17.9% → 20.6%, Petro Chemicals decelerated from 27.7% → 19.4%, and Fertiliser improved from -4.5% → -0.3%.
gaps.implication: Pharma has re-accelerated and now leads chemical credit growth. Fertiliser's contraction is moderating rapidly — the sector may return to positive territory within one period. Others shifting to strengthening status signals broad-based chemical sector momentum.

highlight: Petroleum, Coal Products and Nuclear Fuels

gaps.title: Edible Oils surges 33.0% YoY — Sugar and Tea turn negative
gaps.body: Edible Oils & Vanaspati grew 33.0% year-on-year, Others at 13.6%, while Sugar contracted -0.4% and Tea -3.1%. Edible Oils accelerated sharply from 23.9% → 33.0% while Sugar and Tea both turned negative from 4.3% → -0.4% and 0.8% → -3.1%, widening the growth spread from 23.1pp to 36.1pp.
gaps.implication: The food processing credit story has bifurcated — value-added segments like edible oils are booming while traditional commodities face outright contraction. Sugar and Tea shifting to declining status marks a structural break in agricultural processing finance.

gaps.title: Petroleum/Coal leads at 37.8% YoY — 10 sectors strengthening
gaps.body: Petroleum, Coal Products and Nuclear Fuels grew 37.8% year-on-year, followed by All Engineering at 30.8% and Gems & Jewellery at 26.0%. Petroleum/Coal accelerated from 32.5% → 37.8% to take the top spot from Gems & Jewellery (41.4% → 26.0%), while the bottom tier remained positive with Leather at 6.4%, Rubber/Plastic at 7.3%, and Textiles at 8.3%.
gaps.implication: Energy transition financing is now the fastest-growing industry credit segment, overtaking export-oriented gems and engineering. With 10 sectors strengthening and none declining, industry credit momentum is broad-based and accelerating. Chemicals and Paper shifting to strengthening status confirms the manufacturing credit cycle is deepening.

gaps.title: Ports surge 74.0% YoY — Railways down 44.1%
gaps.body: Ports grew 74.0% year-on-year, Power at 21.5%, and Other Infrastructure at 1.4%, while Railways contracted -44.1%, Airports -26.0%, and Telecommunications -11.9%. Ports accelerated sharply from 48.7% → 74.0% while Power decelerated from 22.1% → 21.5%, Roads from 1.1% → active status, and Railways deepened its contraction from -50.1% → -44.1%.
gaps.implication: Export infrastructure financing is exploding — ports are seeing the fastest credit growth in the entire industry dataset. Power shifting to active status signals the renewable energy credit boom may be plateauing. Railways' contraction is moderating but remains severe, confirming banks are avoiding non-Indian Railways transport projects.
